title: Discordant Aortic Stenosis Indices
presentation: A 78-year-old presents for preoperative echocardiographic assessment prior to noncardiac surgery. TTE shows AV Vmax 3.6 m/s, mean gradient 32 mmHg, and a calculated aortic valve area of 0.85 cm-squared. LVEF is 60%.
interpretation: The Vmax (3.6 m/s) and mean gradient (32 mmHg) suggest moderate AS by Doppler indices, but the calculated AVA (0.85 cm-squared) falls in the severe range (<1.0 cm-squared). This is a discordant case. With a normal LVEF and no evidence of reduced transvalvular flow stated, the first priority is to re-check all measurements — particularly LVOT diameter measured at the correct anatomic level (cusp insertion) and confirmation that CW Doppler was obtained from multiple windows including the right parasternal/suprasternal approach.

decisionPoint.question: What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Accept the AVA and classify as severe AS
Re-verify LVOT diameter measurement and Doppler technique before reclassifying
Proceed directly to surgical referral without further workup
Disregard the AVA entirely and use only the Vmax
rationaleByOption: Incorrect — discordant indices should prompt re-checking measurements before accepting either value, since LVOT diameter error is a major source of AVA miscalculation due to the squared relationship in the area calculation.

Correct — per the BSE 2021 approach to discordant AS indices, the first step is to re-check all measurements, especially LVOT diameter at the correct anatomic level and confirming Doppler was obtained from all windows including non-standard ones.
Incorrect — surgical referral decisions should not be made on unverified, internally discordant data.
Incorrect — both Vmax/gradient and AVA carry diagnostic value; neither should be disregarded without first checking for measurement error.

teachingPearl: When Doppler indices and AVA disagree, technical/measurement error is the most common explanation — re-verify LVOT diameter (measured at cusp insertion, not 1 cm below) and confirm CW Doppler was obtained from multiple acoustic windows before concluding the discordance reflects a genuine low-flow state.
